original_hypothesis.title: Anyone can grief withdrawals by manipulating nextWithdrawal progress with a reverting/hostile ManagedNFT, stalling revenue collection
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: withdrawFromManagedNFTs() is public and iterates ManagedNFTs calling external `getThresholds()` and `withdrawBalancesTo()`. If any ManagedNFT in the list reverts (e.g., not actually owned/approved, malicious contract at that address, or legitimate NFT with misconfigured thresholds causing downstream token transfer revert), the whole withdrawal batch reverts and nextWithdrawal does not advance. This allows indefinite stalling of revenue aggregation if any ManagedNFT is broken; since anyone can call the function, an attacker can repeatedly waste gas and keep the system from making progress if operators rely on public calls and there's no skip mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Revenue aggregation is a core flow. The code has no per-NFT try/catch or skip-on-failure, and ManagedNFTs can be set in constructor without ownership validation. A single bad entry can block harvesting across the portfolio.
deployment_assumptions: ManagedNFTs contains at least one address that will revert on getThresholds() or withdrawBalancesTo() (e.g., stale/unowned/malicious/non-NFT)
No off-chain operator workaround exists to remove/repair the entry promptly
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LiquidInfrastructureERC20 constructor assigns `ManagedNFTs = _managedNFTs` without validating ownership; withdrawFromManagedNFTs() does not handle per-item failures.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawFromAllManagedNFTs(): calls withdrawFromManagedNFTs(ManagedNFTs.length) with no access control; underlying loop makes external calls to each ManagedNFT without try/catch

preconditions: At least one ManagedNFT entry is invalid or reverts on withdrawal path
LockedForDistribution is false (withdraw is blocked during distribution)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: public batch external calls across an owner-controlled list without failure isolation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: single failing item blocks batch progress; no way to skip failing index besides modifying ManagedNFTs (owner-only)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch; no per-NFT success tracking; no admin function to set nextWithdrawal manually

analysis_questions: Can nextWithdrawal become stuck permanently if a particular index always reverts?
What happens if ManagedNFTs contains an EOA or non-NFT contract—does the call revert or return garbage and then revert later?
Is there any off-chain assumed operator that can always call owner-only releaseManagedNFT to remove the bad entry, and is that safe during partial withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Unit test with a malicious NFT that reverts on withdrawBalancesTo() and confirm the entire batch reverts and progress cannot be made
Test behavior when ManagedNFTs includes a non-contract/EOA address
Assess whether partial withdrawals can still succeed by choosing numWithdrawals to avoid the failing index (and whether nextWithdrawal cursor makes that impossible)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If ManagedNFTs accidentally includes a broken address, an attacker can repeatedly call withdrawFromAllManagedNFTs() (or any batch size that hits the bad index) to consume operator time/resources and prevent revenue aggregation from completing. Since distributions depend on aggregated balances, yield payout cadence is disrupted.
prerequisites: At least one failing ManagedNFT entry (misconfiguration or malicious)
Protocol relies on this on-chain function for harvesting

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Revenue remains stranded in NFTs; delayed/blocked distributions; potential missed yield for all holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can remove/fix ManagedNFTs entry, but may be operationally slow and risky if mid-cycle

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for invalid ManagedNFTs entries or revert isolation in withdrawals.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Repeated WithdrawalStarted without progress; revert traces at same ManagedNFT index

validation_result.mechanism: Missing failure isolation in batch external calls with sequential cursor
validation_result.impact_summary: A single reverting ManagedNFT entry permanently blocks the withdrawal cycle at that index, preventing revenue collection from ALL subsequent NFTs until the owner removes the entry. Combined with the `require(true)` bug in `releaseManagedNFT`, stale entries can easily accumulate.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `withdrawFromManagedNFTs` function (lines 262-303) iterates over ManagedNFTs using a sequential cursor (`nextWithdrawal`) and makes two external calls per NFT: `getThresholds()` and `withdrawBalancesTo()`. Neither call is wrapped in try/catch. If any call reverts, the entire transaction reverts and `nextWithdrawal` is not advanced (it's only set at line 287, after the loop). Since the cursor always starts iteration from `nextWithdrawal`, there is no way to skip a broken index — every call that includes the broken index will revert.

The function is public (no access control), so anyone can invoke it, but the inability to advance past a broken entry means anyone who calls the function (including automated bots or operators) will simply waste gas. The owner's only recourse is to call `releaseManagedNFT` to remove the broken entry, but this function itself has a `require(true)` bug (line 399) that can lead to silent failures and stale entries.

Multiple scenarios can cause an NFT to revert:
1. Constructor-injected NFTs (not validated for ownership) will fail the `_isApprovedOrOwner` check in `withdrawBalancesTo`
2. Stale entries from the `require(true)` bug in `releaseManagedNFT`
3. An ERC20 token referenced in thresholds being paused/blacklisted
4. Duplicate entries from repeated `addManagedNFT` calls where one copy is released
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 274-286 confirm external calls without try/catch. Line 287 confirms cursor update only after successful loop completion. Lines 289-292 confirm the only cycle-reset condition requires processing ALL entries. Lines 393-399 confirm the `require(true)` bug that can leave stale entries. The constructor at line 422 confirms `ManagedNFTs = _managedNFTs` without ownership validation.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: External calls to getThresholds() and withdrawBalancesTo() at lines 281-283 are not wrapped in try/catch. If any ManagedNFT at the current nextWithdrawal index reverts, the entire transaction reverts and nextWithdrawal cannot advance past that index. There is no skip mechanism.

The `require(true)` at line 399 always passes regardless of whether the NFT was found in ManagedNFTs. If the NFT address is not in the array, the function succeeds silently, potentially leaving stale entries. This contributes to the withdrawal stalling issue by allowing inconsistent ManagedNFTs state.
Constructor accepts _managedNFTs without verifying this contract owns any of them (unlike addManagedNFT which checks ownership). Any invalid entry would block withdrawals at that index.

calling_sequence: 3. Loop starts at nextWithdrawal (line 274), casts ManagedNFTs[i] to LiquidInfrastructureNFT (line 275-277)
4. External call: withdrawFrom.getThresholds() at line 281 - reverts if address is not a valid contract or doesn't implement interface
5. External call: withdrawFrom.withdrawBalancesTo(withdrawERC20s, address(this)) at line 282 - reverts if ERC20 contract doesn't own the NFT's AccountId token
6. If step 4 or 5 reverts, the entire transaction reverts. nextWithdrawal (line 287) is never updated.
7. All subsequent calls starting from the same nextWithdrawal will hit the same reverting entry
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When nextWithdrawal points to an index with a broken ManagedNFT (e.g., one the contract doesn't own, a stale entry from the require(true) bug, or a non-contract address from constructor), every call to withdrawFromManagedNFTs that would process that index will revert. The cursor never advances because `nextWithdrawal = i` at line 287 is after the loop. The only remediation is owner calling releaseManagedNFT to remove the bad entry, but releaseManagedNFT itself has the require(true) bug and also modifies the array during a partial withdrawal which can corrupt the cursor (INV-STATE-003).

deployment_assumptions_checked: ManagedNFTs contains at least one address that will revert on getThresholds() or withdrawBalancesTo()
No off-chain operator workaround exists to remove/repair the entry promptly
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script at contract-deployer.ts line ~165 deploys LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with an empty _managedNFTs array (`[]`). NFTs are added later via `addManagedNFT` (which validates ownership) and `transferNftToErc20AndManage`. However, the code defect exists independently: stale entries can arise from the `require(true)` bug in releaseManagedNFT, or from external circumstances like ERC20 tokens in thresholds being paused.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The hypothesis is partially correct. While an external attacker cannot directly inject a malicious ManagedNFT (onlyOwner guards addManagedNFT), the code defect is real: (1) the constructor allows unvalidated entries, (2) the `require(true)` bug in releaseManagedNFT can create stale entries pointing to NFTs no longer owned by the contract, (3) no try/catch means any single reverting NFT blocks the entire withdrawal pipeline. The 'grief' aspect is that anyone can waste gas calling the public function knowing it will revert, but the real impact is operational: the protocol cannot collect revenue from ANY NFT beyond the stuck index until the owner intervenes.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH. Revenue collection from all ManagedNFTs at indices >= the broken entry is blocked. Since distributions depend on aggregated balances, delayed revenue collection means delayed or reduced distributions for all ERC20 holders. The `require(true)` bug makes this more likely to occur in practice. Owner can remediate but must identify the issue and call releaseManagedNFT, which itself has bugs (require(true), cursor corruption if mid-withdrawal).
recommended_mitigations: Wrap external calls in withdrawFromManagedNFTs with try/catch to skip failing NFTs and continue processing
Fix require(true) in releaseManagedNFT to use a boolean 'found' flag: `bool found = false; ... found = true; ... require(found, "...");`
Add a guard in releaseManagedNFT and addManagedNFT to prevent modification when nextWithdrawal > 0
Add an admin function to manually reset nextWithdrawal to 0 as an emergency escape hatch
Validate all ManagedNFTs in the constructor using the same ownership check as addManagedNFT
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with empty ManagedNFTs. 2. Deploy two LiquidInfrastructureNFTs, transfer both to ERC20, call addManagedNFT for each. 3. Transfer one NFT away directly (or create a stale entry via releaseManagedNFT with wrong address exploiting require(true)). 4. Call withdrawFromAllManagedNFTs() - observe revert at the stale entry. 5. Verify nextWithdrawal has not advanced. 6. Confirm subsequent calls also revert.
uncertainties: Whether any real deployment will pass non-empty _managedNFTs to the constructor (deployment script passes empty, but other deployments may differ)
Whether operator monitoring would quickly detect and fix a stuck withdrawal cycle in production
additional_testing_needed: Test withdrawFromManagedNFTs with a ManagedNFT that reverts on getThresholds()
Test withdrawFromManagedNFTs with a ManagedNFT that reverts on withdrawBalancesTo() (not owned)
Test releaseManagedNFT with an address not in ManagedNFTs to confirm require(true) passes silently
Test interaction between releaseManagedNFT and nextWithdrawal when mid-cycle
